title: Wavelet-based multiresolution analysis of quantum fractals in confined dynamics
abstract: Fractal structures naturally emerge in quantum systems whose initial states exhibit spatial discontinuities, a phenomenon first identified by Berry in the paradigmatic case of a particle confined in an infinite potential well. While previous analyses of quantum fractals have mainly relied on spectral decompositions and geometric scaling arguments, their quantitative characterization often depends on scale choices and truncation effects. Here we present a wavelet-based multiresolution framework that enables a direct and assumption-free quantification of quantum fractality. Fractal dimensions are extracted from the scale-dependent distribution of wavelet energies, without invoking prior power-law hypotheses. The method is applied to space and time quantum fractals arising in confined dynamics, as well as to dynamical curves generated by the associated quantum probability flux. These flux-driven trajectories provide a natural space--time parametrization of the underlying fractal structure and yield scaling properties fully consistent with Berry's predictions for space--time fractals. The resulting fractal dimensions are shown to be robust with respect to the choice of wavelet family, numerical cutoffs, and system parameters. Beyond validating earlier conjectures, the present framework offers a unified and computationally efficient tool for the multiscale analysis of quantum fractality in confined and interference-driven quantum dynamics. That is, it provides an operational, scale-adaptive criterion that unifies the characterization of space, time, and space--time quantum fractals within a single, hypothesis-free approach.
summary: This paper introduces a new wavelet-based method to accurately measure the fractal dimensions of quantum interference patterns (quantum carpets) without needing prior assumptions about scaling laws. The method is shown to be highly robust and recovers established theoretical predictions for space, time, and space-time fractals. It offers a unified, computationally efficient tool for analyzing multiscale dynamics in confined quantum systems.
main_problem: The quantitative characterization of quantum fractals (quantum carpets) is difficult because traditional methods like box-counting or Fourier analysis are sensitive to numerical cutoffs and require prior assumptions about scaling ranges.
main_result: The authors developed a wavelet-based multiresolution analysis (MRA) framework that provides a robust, hypothesis-free, and scale-adaptive way to extract fractal dimensions, successfully recovering Berry's predicted values.
method: A wavelet-based multiresolution framework using discrete wavelet transforms (Haar and Daubechies families) to analyze the scale-dependent distribution of wavelet energies and Bohmian trajectories as adaptive probes.
model_or_system: A quantum particle confined in a one-dimensional infinite potential well, starting from initial states with spatial discontinuities (e.g., square wave functions).
key_observables: Fractal dimensions (space, time, and space-time), wavelet energies, probability density, and quantum probability flux trajectories.
important_parameters: Dyadic scales (j), spectral truncation (N), recurrence time (T), and Hurst exponent (H).
main_assumptions: The system is inherently 'pre-fractal' due to finite spectral truncation; the use of Bohmian trajectories is for methodological probing rather than ontological interpretation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows quantum carpets with superimposed Bohmian trajectories; Figure 2 shows spatial density profiles and convergence of fractal dimensions vs. truncation; Figure 3 shows temporal profiles and convergence; Figure 4 shows convergence for flux-based trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of fractal characterization, presents the wavelet-based MRA framework, applies it to spatial and temporal profiles of quantum carpets, validates the method using Bohmian trajectories, and demonstrates robustness across different wavelet families and parameters.
why_interesting: This provides a new, robust diagnostic tool for analyzing complex interference patterns and self-similar structures in any system exhibiting wave-like interference and confinement, such as ultracold atoms in optical lattices or wave-packet dynamics in cavities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fractal space–time quantum carpets generated by the evolution in time of the probability density associated with (a) a coherent superposition of two symmetrically distributed square functions, and (b) only the left-hand side square function. In both cases, the width of the square functions is such that w/L = 0.25 and are centered at x± = ±0.25L. In the density plots, blue denotes lower values of the probability density, while the transition to red indicates increasing values (for a better visualization of the details both probability densities ) For a better visualization of the carpets’ details, the probability densities have been truncated to about 60% their maxima. FIG. 3. Time quantum fractal profile exhibited by the prob- ability density of the symmetric (a) and asymmetric (b) wave functions shown in Fig. 1. Both time fractals are computed at x = −0.25L, where L denotes the total length of the cavity. The numerical estimates of the corresponding fractal dimen- sions obtained from the multiresolution analysis are shown, respectively, in panels (c) and (d): Haar (black), db4 (red), db8 (green), and db16 (blue). These estimates are determined from convergence with the number of basis functions consid- ered in the spectral reconstruction without adding any extra hypothesis. As it is shown, beyond N ≳100, all results show fast convergence to the value D...
FIG. 2. Space quantum fractal profile exhibited by the prob- ability density of the symmetric (a) and asymmetric (b) wave functions shown in Fig. 1. Both space fractals are computed at t = T/ √

FIG. 4. Numerical estimates of the fractal dimension ob- tained from the multiresolution analysis for three flux-based fractal (Bohmian) trajectories for different instances: Haar (black), db4 (red), db8 (green), and db16 (blue). The re- sults in panels (a) and (b) correspond to trajectories with initial conditions taken at x0 = −0.25L associated with the symmetric and asymmetric wave functions whose carpets are shown in Figs. 1(a) and (b), respectively, with w/L = 0.25. Note that, beyond N ≳100, all results show fast convergence to the value D = 1.25 (magenta dashed line) conjectured by Berry for space–time fractals [3].
